front: Architecture: four identical temple porticos, one facing each cardinal direction, surround a central circular domed hall
Architecture: Thomas Jefferson used it as the primary model for Monticello; built for Paolo Almerico outside Vicenza
Architecture: central domed hall inspired by the Roman Pantheon; building is oriented 45 degrees from the cardinal directions so every room receives sunlight
Architecture: inspired Lord Burlington's Chiswick House in England; Le Corbusier's Villa Savoye was also inspired by it
Architecture: small rooms flanking two of its entrances break its strict symmetry; dome changed from hemisphere to a flatter oculus form, later covered by a cupola
Architecture: Palladio classified it as a palace because of its proximity to the city; praised its 'most exquisite views' giving 'the appearance of a grand theater'
Architecture: 1570 treatise by Palladio; Thomas Jefferson called it his 'Bible'; covers the five classical orders, city planning, and ends with descriptions of 26 Roman temples
back: The Four Books of Architecture (Andrea Palladio)
work: The Four Books of Architecture (I Quattro Libri dell'Architettura)

back: The Four Books of Architecture (Andrea Palladio)
work: The Four Books of Architecture (I Quattro Libri dell'Architettura)

front: Architecture: claims dimensions should follow mathematical ratios also found in musical intervals, satisfying a universal mathematical order
back: The Four Books of Architecture (Andrea Palladio)
work: The Four Books of Architecture (I Quattro Libri dell'Architettura)

front: Architecture: Palladio illustrated Daniele Barbaro's commentary on Vitruvius; Colen Campbell called Palladio 'the best of the moderns' in the preface to Vitruvius Britannicus
back: The Four Books of Architecture (Andrea Palladio)
work: The Four Books of Architecture (I Quattro Libri dell'Architettura)

front: Architecture: became popular in England after the Earl of Arundel and Inigo Jones traveled to Italy
back: The Four Books of Architecture (Andrea Palladio)
work: The Four Books of Architecture (I Quattro Libri dell'Architettura)

front: Architecture: Palladio's final commission; after his death, student Vincenzo Scamozzi completed it and added a trompe l'oeil stage set depicting streets receding upstage
Architecture: Palladio designed the loggia of the Basilica Palladiana in Vicenza with tripartite arched openings called Palladian windows or Serlian windows
Architecture: white marble facade featuring a four-column elevated temple front superimposed over a second, lower temple facade; designed to be seen from across the water in Venice
Architecture: Tintoretto's diagonal Last Supper hangs inside; facade was completed by Vincenzo Scamozzi after Palladio's death
Architecture: Palladio built on the geometric principles of Alberti for this monastery church; his planned oculus over the portal was never realized
Architecture: white stone mansion on the Brenta Riviera; raised front portico accessible via twin side stairways; nicknamed 'La Malcontenta' from a legend that the owner's wife was locked inside
Architecture: built for brothers Nicolo and Alvise Foscari; interior frescoes by Giambattista Zelotti
Architecture: built for Daniele Barbaro; Veronese painted frescoes including Abundance, Fortitude, and Fury, and an octagonal ceiling fresco of seven Olympians surrounding a woman riding a snake
Architecture: two symmetrical wings lead out to pavilions with sundials beneath the pediments
Architect: born Andrea di Pietro della Gondola; nickname 'Palladio' given by patron Gian Giorgio Trissino after Pallas Athena
Architect: active in and around Vicenza, Italy; Vicenza designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site to commemorate his buildings
Architect: Inigo Jones introduced his style to England; Jones's Banqueting House at Whitehall was the first consciously classical building in Britain
Architect: commissioned to create a triumphal arch for Henry III's visit to Venice in 1574
Architect: many buildings feature a granary above the main bedrooms, reflecting the Venetian custom of storing goods in attics rather than marshy basements
Architect: urban palazzi inspired by Bramante's Palazzo Caprini; Il Redentore church in Venice has two minaret-like bell towers
Architect: Rudolf Wittkower wrote about him in Architectural Principles in the Age of Humanism; Karl Jenkins composed a string suite titled 'Palladio' used in De Beers ads
Architect: championed the Tuscan/Etruscan column style as representing core Italian tradition; Palazzo Chiericati uses Doric columns on the ground floor
